title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman recounts how 19th-century abolitionists used nullification, jury nullification, and state Personal Liberty Acts to defy the Fugitive Slave Act, then argues that modern legislators like Greg Treat invert history when they call nullifiers the 'villains.' The article frames nullification as the only morally legitimate response to unconstitutional federal evils such as the Fugitive Slave Act and Roe v. Wade.
audiences: constitutional conservatives and tenth amendment proponents

questions.question: What concrete 19th-century episode does the article use to show abolitionists nullifying the Fugitive Slave Act in practice?
questions.answer: It centers on the 1854 Joshua Glover case in Wisconsin, where a mob of abolitionists broke Glover out of the Milwaukee jail, sent him to Canada via the Underground Railroad, and the Wisconsin Supreme Court later acquitted Sherman Booth by ruling the Fugitive Slave Act unconstitutional, effectively nullifying the federal law in Wisconsin.
questions.quote: Paine, the author of the *habeas* *corpus* writ, defended Booth in court by arguing the Fugitive Slave Act was unconstitutional and therefore void. The State Supreme Court agreed and acquitted Booth. The Fugitive Slave Act had been nullified in Wisconsin.

questions.question: How did northern state legislatures use statutory nullification against the Fugitive Slave Act?
questions.answer: Vermont's 1850 Habeas Corpus Act required the state to defend anyone claimed as a fugitive slave, Michigan passed a near-identical Personal Freedom Act, and Massachusetts' 1855 Personal Liberty Act criminalized enforcement of the Fugitive Slave Act with up to five years in prison and disbarred attorneys who helped slave-catchers.
questions.quote: Massachusetts' Personal Liberty Act of 1855 made enforcement of the Fugitive Slave Act a crime punishable by a sentence of up to five years. Further, it mandated impeachment for any state official who aided slave-catchers and disbarred all attorneys assisting in the return of slaves to their "owners."

questions.question: Which prominent abolitionist voices explicitly identified themselves with nullification?
questions.answer: Poet John Greenleaf Whittier publicly declared himself a 'nullifier' regarding the Fugitive Slave Law, and William Lloyd Garrison endorsed the position in The Liberator, calling such nullification 'loyalty to goodness.'
questions.quote: "So far as that law is concerned, I am a nullifier." Abolitionist leader William Lloyd Garrison concurred in an article for *The Liberator*: "The nullification advocated by Mr Whittier…is loyalty to goodness."

questions.question: Who actually opposed nullification in the antebellum period, according to the article?
questions.audience: constitutional conservatives and tenth amendment proponents
questions.answer: Slave owners, slave-state newspapers like the New Orleans Bee, and figures such as Senator (and later Confederate President) Jefferson Davis bitterly opposed northern nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act, viewing it as a threat to the institution of slavery.
questions.quote: In the years preceding slavery's abolition, nullification was practiced exclusively by abolitionists to the acute dismay of the slave states. To oppose nullification in the context of American history is to side with the slavers.

questions.question: How does Jefferson Davis distinguish nullification from secession in the quote Silberman uses?
questions.audience: constitutional conservatives and tenth amendment proponents
questions.answer: Davis treats them as opposites: nullification is a remedy applied within the Union against a federal agent that has exceeded its constitutional authority, while secession is reserved for cases where the states themselves have violated the constitutional rights of other states.
questions.quote: "Nullification and secession, so often confounded, are indeed, antagonistic principles. Nullification is a remedy which it is sought to apply within the Union, and against the agent of the States. It is only to be justified then the agent has violated his constitutional obligations…"

questions.question: What hypocrisy does Silberman charge Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat with?
questions.answer: Treat publicly attacked nullification as the 'villains of the stories' and the 'wrong side of history' while killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, even though he had himself authored a 2016 bill nullifying federal EPA Clean Power Plan regulations.
questions.quote: There's hypocrisy here as Treat [authored a bill](https://blog.tenthamendmentcenter.com/2016/03/oklahoma-senate-passes-bill-to-block-implementation-of-epa-clean-power-plan-rules/) nullifying federal regulations in 2016, but worse are the lies he's spreading about American history which are exactly opposite of the truth. Nullifiers were the heroes – their opponents the villains.

questions.question: Why does Silberman draw a moral parallel between Roe v. Wade and the Fugitive Slave Act / Dred Scott?
questions.answer: He argues Roe is just as wicked and just as unconstitutional as Dred Scott or the Fugitive Slave Act because all three rest on separating personhood from humanness, and Article VI of the Constitution makes federal action not pursuant to the Constitution illegitimate, so states have a duty to nullify Roe.
questions.quote: Roe v Wade is no less wicked than Scott v Sanford or the Fugitive Slave Act. The legalizations of both abortion and slavery were grounded in the separation of personhood from humanness, which is the pinnacle of injustice.

questions.question: What moral framing does Silberman give to state governments that refuse to nullify unjust federal law?
questions.answer: He calls submission to unconstitutional federal evil 'cowardice or idolatry' and explicitly compares the 'just following orders' defense of compliant state governments to the rejected defense of mid-level Nazis at Nuremberg.
questions.quote: Faced with evils such as these, nullification is the only appropriate response. Anything less is cowardice or idolatry. "I was just following orders" was no excuse for mid-level Nazis in Nuremberg and it's no excuse for state governments now.

questions.question: How was jury nullification used alongside legislative nullification by abolitionists?
questions.answer: After roughly 2,500 abolitionists broke William 'Jerry' Henry out of custody in Syracuse in 1851, 25 of the 26 defendants tried for assisting his escape were acquitted by juries refusing to convict under the Fugitive Slave Act, and Vermont abolitionists similarly counted on local juries to free captured fugitives.
questions.quote: 26 were tried for helping Jerry escape, and only one convicted. The other 25 were acquitted due to jury nullification being employed against federal law.

questions.question: Why does the author claim most Americans have never heard this nullification history?
questions.answer: He blames a deliberate 'rewriting of history by statists' and the unwillingness of government schools and textbook authors to disseminate information that would undermine a federal-government-good versus nullifiers-bad narrative.
questions.quote: Unfortunately, a rewriting of history by statists has cut Americans off from the knowledge of these nullifiers who acted heroically in defiance of evil. That's a sacrifice most government schools and textbook authors are willing to make lest they disseminate information that threatens the narrative of federal-government-good, racist-crazy-eyed-nullifiers-bad.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Abolitionists Rising's standing reference page on nullification, providing the movement's current doctrinal position that Silberman's historical case supports.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Silberman's companion piece from the same month attacking Treat for killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, the very episode quoted in this article.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Continues the debate over Treat's misrepresentation of nullification, distinguishing it from secession exactly as Jefferson Davis does in the quoted passage.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Another case study of a state legislator dismissing nullification, offering a parallel to the Treat hypocrisy described here.

related.title: Greg Treat's Mind-Numbingly Ignorant Opposition to HB1236
related.reason: Follow-on critique of Treat's ongoing opposition to nullification-based abolition bills in Oklahoma.

related.title: Randy Brogdon: Righteous Indignation in Response to New York's New Law
related.reason: Frames nullification as the proper state response to federal/state abortion law, applying the same logic Silberman draws from the Fugitive Slave Act fight.
